title: Client Report -How good is it, really?

QUESTION 1

Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics. You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.

before 1980 Accuracy - the percentage of how often the predictions are correct precision - the amount that are true/predicted to be true recall - gives you the actual number from the data to compare
I think that with this dataset, it is more important to have precision in your data. This tells us how much of the data that was projected to be correct is actually correct. In this case, it tells us how many of the of the houses estimated to have been built before 1980 were actually built before 1980.
This tells us that there is a 94% chance that the positive data is correct.

text:
QUESTION

Take time to get to know your data. Based on some initial exploration, identify 3 variables in the dataset you think will have strong predictive ability for determining if a house was built before 1980. Explain/justify the reasoning behind your guesses (for example with plots).

Based on the data, Some variables that I would think have a strong predictive ability are the condition columns for one. This is because I would think that the newer homes are in a better condition that those that are older.
I also think that the size of the garage would be bigger for more current homes, as people like to have more storage space, and more cars in their homes.
The last variable I would like is the number of floors. I think that older houses are more likely to have one floor and maybe a basement, while newer houses are more likely to build up.

Build a classification model labeling houses as being built “before 1980” or “during or after 1980”. Your goal is to reach or exceed 90% accuracy. Report your final model choice and any other model parameters you may have tweaked (train-test split ratio, tuning parameters, etc).

The first thing that I did here was remove the columns that are already predicted, and the identifier column. This removes the columns that will effect the outcome. I then set the test data to 20%, with 80% being used for training. I used the random forest classifier with 200 estimators. This got me to 93% accuracy.

Write an SQL query that pulls the tables you need into a polars dataframe. Then, use python polars to create a summary table showing the average salary for each position (e.g., pitcher, catcher, outfielder). Position information can be found in the fielding table in the POS column. Include the following columns:
position average_salary total_players highest_salary The highest_salary column should display the highest salary ever earned by a player in that position.
Additionally, add a new column to the summary table you just created to label the average salary:
If the average salary is above $3 million, categorize it as “High Salary.” If the average salary is between $2 million and $3 million, categorize it as “Medium Salary.” Otherwise, categorize it as “Low Salary.” Order the table by average salary in descending order.
Hint: Beware, it is common for a player to play multiple positions in a single year. For this analysis, each player’s salary should only be counted toward one position in a given year. You get to determine which position to count it towards.


Show the code
import polars as pl
import sqlite3

sqlite_file = "lahmansbaseballdb.sqlite"
con = sqlite3.connect(sqlite_file)

query = """
SELECT
    s.playerID,
    s.yearID,
    s.salary,
    f.POS,
    f.G
FROM Salaries s
JOIN Fielding f
ON s.playerID = f.playerID AND s.yearID = f.yearID
"""

df = pl.read_database(query, con)



df_positions = (
    df.sort(by="G", descending=True)
    .unique(subset=["playerID", "yearID"], keep="first")
    .select([
        pl.col("playerID"),
        pl.col("yearID"),
        pl.col("POS").alias("position"),
        pl.col("salary")
    ])
)

summary = (
    df_positions
    .group_by("position")
    .agg([
        pl.mean("salary").alias("average_salary"),
        pl.n_unique("playerID").alias("total_players"),
        pl.max("salary").alias("highest_salary")
    ])
)

summary = summary.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("average_salary") > 3_000_000)
    .then(pl.lit("High Salary"))
    .when(pl.col("average_salary") >= 2_000_000)
    .then(pl.lit("Medium Salary"))
    .otherwise(pl.lit("Low Salary"))
    .alias("salary_category")
)

mysummary = summary.sort("average_salary", descending=True)

QUESTION 1
Write an SQL query that provides playerID, yearID, and batting average for players with at least 1 at bat that year. Sort the table from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
This shows the top 5 players’ batting averages for the year if they batted at least once. the top five in this case have 1 for their average because they only batted once.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here

# The code below can serve as a template for all the rest of your queries. It is just missing the
# SQL code. Put your SQL query in between the triple quotation marks to create the query. The records will be stored in 'results', which is a polars data frame

q = """
SELECT
    playerID,
    yearID,
    CAST(SUM(H) AS FLOAT) / SUM(AB) AS batting_avg
FROM batting
WHERE AB >= 1
GROUP BY playerID, yearID
HAVING SUM(AB) >= 1
ORDER BY batting_avg DESC, playerID ASC
LIMIT 5;
    """
results = pl.read_database(q, con)

text:
QUESTION 1
Use the provided code above to read in the batting table. Then, with Python Polars manipulate the ‘results’ dataframe to calculate the number of at bats and the batting average for each year a player played. Filter the data frame to only include players with at least 10 at bats that year. Sort the dataframe from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
Note: A batting average is meant to measure how often a player puts the ball in play and gets to base safely. For this task, use a simplified version of batting average defined as the sum of hits (labeled ‘H’) divided by number of at bats (labeled ‘AB’).
With this table, we can see that the top 5 averages are all over .5, but their years are widely spread, showing that there are no time factors in the increasing of batting averages of players.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q = """
    SELECT *
    FROM batting
    """
results = pl.read_database(q, con, infer_schema_length=None) 
# What does infer_schema_length = None do?

results_1 = (
    results
    .group_by(["playerID", "yearID"])
    .agg(
        AB = pl.col("AB").sum(),
        H = pl.col("H").sum()

title: Client Report - Flights: Column Aggregation
text:
QUESTION 2

Which airport has the worst delays? Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the “worst” airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours.

I think that the percentage of flights that are delayed is the most important for finding the “worst” airport. In this case I divided the delayed flights with the number of flights total and multiplied by 100 to get a percentage of flights. This makes “SFO” the worst airport.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
summary = (
    df
    .group_by("airport_code")
    .agg(
        pl.sum("num_of_flights_total").alias("total_flights"),
        pl.sum("num_of_delays_total").alias("delayed_flights"),
        pl.sum("minutes_delayed_total").alias("total_delay_minutes")
    )
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("delayed_flights") / pl.col("total_flights"))
            .alias("prop_delayed"),
        (pl.col("total_delay_minutes") / pl.col("delayed_flights") / 60)
            .alias("avg_delay_hours")
    )
    .select(
        "airport_code",
        "total_flights",
        "delayed_flights",
        "prop_delayed",
        "avg_delay_hours"
    )
    .sort(["prop_delayed", "avg_delay_hours"], descending=[True, True])
)

title: Client Report - Flights: Column Aggregation
text:
QUESTION 4
When controlling for airport and month, what is the trend in delayed flights over the years? For each row, calculate a ratio: number of delayed flights over the average number of delayed flights at that airport in that month. Then create a polished boxplot of the results, with year along the x axis. What does a point above 1 represent and what does a point below 1 represent? What insights do you gain from this plot? What trend do you see, if any? Is it a good trend or bad trend?
1 represents a center of the average. The more points above 1 represents the more flights that are delayed. As the years go on, you can see the boxes get smaller and closer to the line, meaning an improvement in consistancy.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
avg_delays = (
    df
    .group_by(["airport_code", "month"])
    .agg(
        pl.mean("num_of_delays_total").alias("avg_monthly_delays")
    )
)
df_ratio = df.join(
    avg_delays,
    on=["airport_code", "month"]
)
df_ratio = df_ratio.with_columns(
    (pl.col("num_of_delays_total") / pl.col("avg_monthly_delays"))
    .alias("delay_ratio")
)
df_ratio = df_ratio.filter(pl.col("year").is_not_null())

#boxplot
ggplot(
    df_ratio,
    aes(x="year", y="delay_ratio")
) + \
    geom_boxplot() + \
    geom_hline(yintercept=1, linetype="dashed") + \
    scale_x_continuous(format="d") + \
    labs(
        title="Relative Flight Delays by Year",
        x="Year",
        y="Delay Ratio"
    ) + \
    theme_minimal()

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.
10,444 babies were named Rebecca in the year 2002, when the name began to be on a down slope.


Show the code
# Q1
from lets_plot import *
LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

me = "Rebecca"
birthyear = 2002
state_cols = df.columns[2:]

Rebecca_df = (
    df
    .filter(pl.col("name") == me)
    .with_columns(total_babies=pl.sum_horizontal(state_cols))
    .select(["year", "total_babies"])
    .sort("year")
)

x = [str(v) for v in Rebecca_df["year"].to_list()]
y = Rebecca_df["total_babies"].to_list()
birth_total = Rebecca_df.filter(pl.col("year") == birthyear)["total_babies"][0]

plot = (
    ggplot({'year': x, 'total': y})
    + geom_line(aes(x='year', y='total'), color="blue", size=1)
    + geom_point(aes(x=[str(birthyear)], y=[birth_total]), color="red", size=4)
    + geom_text(
        data={'year': [str(birthyear)], 'total': [birth_total], 'label': [f"{birthyear}: {birth_total}"]},
        mapping=aes(x='year', y='total', label='label'),
        color="red",
        nudge_y=10
    )
    + ggtitle(f"In 2002, 10,444 babies were named Rebecca")
    + xlab("Year")
    + ylab("Number of Babies")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 1

Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.
When I was born in Utah in 2002, the popularity of the name Rebecca was on a downslope in Utah, Idaho, and Arizona in the year that I was born.

# Q1
rebecca_df = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Rebecca")
    .with_columns(
        total_babies = pl.col("ID") + pl.col("AZ") + pl.col("UT")
    )
    .select(["year", "ID", "AZ", "UT", "total_babies"])
    .sort("year")
)

plot = (
    ggplot(rebecca_df, aes(x="year", y="total_babies"))
    + geom_line(color="pink") 
    + geom_vline(xintercept=2002, linetype="dotted", color="blue")
    + geom_text(x=2002, y=450, label="My Birth Year", color="black")
    + labs(
        title="Total Popularity of the Name 'Rebecca'",
        subtitle="Combined total for Idaho, Arizona, and Utah",
        x="Year",
        y="Total Number of Babies"
    )
    +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
)

plot.show()

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 2

Let’s look at a name that is special for members of the church: Brigham. For each year, calculate what proportion of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah? Make a graph displaying the proportion over time.


# Q2
Brigham_df = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Brigham")
    .with_columns(
        total_babies = (pl.col("ID") + pl.col("UT"))/ pl.col('Total')

    .sort("year")
)

plot = (
    ggplot(Brigham_df, aes(x="year", y="total_babies"))

        subtitle="Combined total for Idaho, and Utah",
        x="Year",
        y="Total Number of Babies"

    +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 3

Consider doing some data validation. For the name David, calculate a sum of all 50 states and District of Columbia for each year. Now compare that total to the value in the ‘Total’ column. In which years, if any, do they not agree?

The data is correct, there is no year where the sum of all babies named David does not match the total that is given.

david_validation = (
    df.filter(pl.col("name") == "David")
    .with_columns(
        Manual_State_Sum = pl.sum_horizontal(pl.exclude("name", "year", "Total"))
    )
    .with_columns(
        is_match = pl.col("Manual_State_Sum") == pl.col("Total"),
        difference = pl.col("Manual_State_Sum") - pl.col("Total")
    )
)
disagreement_years = (
    david_validation
    .filter(pl.col("is_match") == False)
    .select(["year", "Total", "Manual_State_Sum", "difference"])
    .sort("year")
)
print(disagreement_years)

QUESTION 2

Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Be creative! Write an SQL query to read in the table(s) you need, then use polars to manipulate the data. Finally, make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?

The Chicago White Sox are my family’s team, as my grandpa is from Chicago himself. My family currently resides in Arizona, home of the diamondbacks. I decided to show the data of the number of Home runs from the start of the Arizona Diamondbacks, back in 1998. From this graph, I can see that while the Sox have declined in recent years, that their home run count is climbing back up from the low in 2015. The Diamondbacks have been relatively constant in their homerun counts, but also plummetted in 2014, and have been climbing again in recent years.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q = """
SELECT 
    yearID, 
    name, 
    teamID,
    HR,
    W
FROM Teams
WHERE (teamID = 'CHA' OR teamID = 'ARI')
  AND yearID >= 1998
ORDER BY yearID ASC
"""

df_teams = pl.read_database(q, con)

df_teams = df_teams.with_columns(
    (pl.col("W") / 162).alias("win_rate") 
)

p = (
    ggplot(df_teams, aes(x='yearID', y='HR', color='name')) +
    geom_line(size=1) +
    geom_point(size=3) +
    scale_color_manual(values=['#000000', '#A71930']) +
    labs(
        title="Home Run Trends: White Sox vs. Diamondbacks",
        subtitle="Arizona vs Chicago, yearly from 1998",
        x="Year",
        y="Total Home Runs",
        color="Team Name"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()
)

p.show()

def perm_importance_table(model, X_test, y_test, feature_names, top_n=5):
    results = permutation_importance(model, X_test, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=17)
    df_imp = pl.DataFrame({
        "feature": feature_names,
        "perm_imp": results.importances_mean
    }).sort("perm_imp", descending=True)
    return df_imp.head(top_n)
